---
title: "About"
---

I came to data science slowly, and mostly by accident. I studied psychology at UC Santa Barbara and left without much of a quantitative foundation; a math post-bacc at CUNY a few years later helped me start to close that gap. Most of what I know I picked up on the job, one role at a time, learning from people further along than me — and I still feel like I'm learning the craft.

## Starting in Support

My first exposure to business data was in support at ZocDoc. I wasn't doing analytics, I was answering tickets — but after enough conversations you start to notice the same issues clustering in ways that aren't always visible upstream. I started pulling ticket data into spreadsheets and sharing the patterns with the product team. That habit stuck: start with what's actually happening, then figure out what to do about it.

## Product Specialist at Square

I joined Square as a product specialist during the hardware transition from swipe to chip to contactless. The job was bridging support and product — understanding where customers were getting stuck and translating that into signals the product team could act on. Over five years I grew into an analytics role. The project I'm most proud of from that time was a classifier that automated a big chunk of support case tagging and freed up real CS capacity. But more than any single project, Square is where I learned how much of the value sits at the seam between functions — where the data is messy, the stakeholders have different goals, and the job is making sure the right evidence reaches the right decision.

I also taught a data analytics course at General Assembly in 2017 — 10 weeks of SQL, statistics, and experimentation with career changers. Teaching people who are seeing this stuff for the first time is the fastest way to find out whether you actually understand it yourself.

## Building DS Chops at Atlassian

I joined Atlassian in 2020 as the first data scientist hire for the events team. The scope was tight enough that I could build the pipeline end-to-end — instrumentation, measurement, experimentation — instead of inheriting someone else's stack. From there I moved into marketing DS, where the problems got bigger: ICP modeling, buying group analysis, propensity scoring for the enterprise GTM org. The same seam-between-functions work I'd learned at Square, but now with real modeling tools behind it.

Toward the end I spent a lot of time on how LLMs might change the shape of DS work itself. My honest read is that they're already a meaningful unlock — they compress the boring parts of the loop so more time can go toward judgment calls. But only if you pair them with the verification today's models still require: checking the join, sanity-checking the numbers, pressure-testing the framing before it reaches a stakeholder. I've seen the tooling work well, and I've seen it quietly produce work that looks right and isn't. I'd rather err on the slow side until the tooling catches up.

A lot of what I ended up doing at Atlassian was helping the rest of the data org find that balance — setting up MCP connections, writing knowledge files, sitting with people through their first workflows. That work became the adoption playbook I've written up separately, and it's one of the parts of the last few years I've enjoyed most. It pulled on the same thing I liked about teaching: watching someone go from "I don't know how this works" to using it comfortably is a hard feeling to beat.

## Where I Am Now

I was laid off from Atlassian in March 2026 as part of a broader restructure. I'm based in the SF Bay Area and looking for senior data science roles where the work is close to the business, the support-to-product-to-DS progression is an asset, and I can keep learning how AI tooling actually changes this kind of work in practice.

## Education

- **B.A. Psychology** — UC Santa Barbara
- **Post-Baccalaureate, Mathematics** — City University of New York
